title: Fekkas
subtitle: Moroccan twice-baked biscuits with anise, sesame and almonds
excerpt: Fekkas are the Moroccan equivalent of biscotti: a log of firm, egg-based dough baked once, sliced while warm, then dried in a low oven until hard and shelf-stable. Anise seeds, toasted sesame and blanched almonds give a flavour that is less sweet than most Western biscuits and very good with mint tea. The whole process takes about 1 hour 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Fekkas are documented throughout Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia as a standard accompaniment to mint tea. Nargisse Benkabbou describes them as a Ramadan staple in 'Casablanca: My Moroccan Food' (2018). Paula Wolfert notes their similarity to Italian biscotti in technique in 'The Food of Morocco' (2011). The anise and sesame combination is consistent across North African recipes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed container for up to 3 months. They do not need thawing before eating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fekkas keep for 3 weeks at room temperature in an airtight tin. The flavour and crunch improve after 2 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: first bake at 180 degrees C for 25 minutes; second bake at 150 degrees C for 20 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of baking a shaped log of firm dough, slicing it while still warm, then baking the slices a second time to dry them completely. The first bake sets the structure; the second removes residual moisture and produces a hard, shelf-stable biscuit. Slicing while warm is important: cold logs crack rather than cut cleanly. The technique appears in North African fekkas, Italian biscotti, Greek paximadia and Provencal croquants.

content.text: : the first bake cooks the dough through; the second, at a lower temperature, drives out moisture and produces the characteristic hard, dry texture. Slice the log while it is still warm from the first bake; a cold log will crack rather than cut cleanly.

Toast the sesame seeds in a dry pan before adding them to the dough. Raw sesame has much less flavour than toasted; 2 to 3 minutes over medium heat until they begin to colour and smell nutty is enough.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Whisk together the 2 eggs, 120 g caster sugar, 80 ml vegetable oil, orange zest and 1 tsp vanilla. Stir in the 300 g flour, 1 tsp baking powder, 1 tbsp anise seeds, 50 g toasted sesame and 80 g chopped almonds to form a firm, slightly sticky dough. Divide in two and shape each half into a log about 25 cm long and 4 cm wide on a lined baking tray. Flatten the top slightly.

Bake at 180 degrees C fan for 22 to 26 minutes until the logs are golden brown on top and firm to the touch. Remove from the oven and leave to cool for 10 minutes only: the log must still be warm for slicing.

Reduce the oven to 150 degrees C fan. Using a sharp serrated knife, slice each log at a slight diagonal into pieces about 8 mm thick. Lay the slices flat on the baking tray. Return to the oven for 18 to 22 minutes, turning once halfway, until the biscuits are dry and lightly golden on both sides, and the almond pieces are deep golden at their edges. Cool completely on a rack before storing.

items.solution: The log was too cool. Slice while it is still warm and slightly flexible; once cold it becomes brittle. Use a sharp serrated knife and a sawing motion rather than pressing straight down.

items.problem: Biscuits are soft after the second bake
items.solution: The second bake was too short or the oven temperature too low. Return them to the oven for a further 10 minutes at 140 degrees C. They will continue to harden as they cool on the rack.

items.problem: Dough was too sticky to shape
items.solution: Flour your hands and the baking tray lightly. The dough is meant to be slightly sticky; adding too much extra flour will make the biscuits tough.
